title: Phase 13 Capstone
concept: Capstone review of video representation, 3D convolutions, optical flow, video architectures, and object detection and tracking in video sequences.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main role of the appearance descriptor in DeepSORT?

options.text: To extract visual features that prevent ID swaps during overlaps
To calculate the speed of the moving object
To double the size of the bounding box

exercises.explain: Yes! Visual features match identities before and after occlusions, preventing ID swaps.

cards.title: You made it!
cards.body: Congratulations! You have covered the full range of Video and Temporal Vision. From analyzing basic frame sequences to mapping pixel movement vectors, inflating 2D networks, and tracking unique objects through crowded spaces. Let's do a quick final check of the key concepts!

cards.title: Temporal Vision Recap
cards.body:
Let's summarize the key building blocks:
• Video: A sequential stack of 3D image volumes over a temporal axis.
• 3D CNNs: Cube filters that slide across space and time.
• Optical Flow: 2D vectors representing pixel displacement between frames.
• Video Classification: Grouping sequences into actions (C3D, Two-Stream, I3D).
• Tracking: Maintaining IDs over trajectories (DeepSORT).

cards.title: The movie production crew
cards.body: Think of temporal vision as a movie production. The video is the film reel. 3D CNNs are the directors looking at scenes. Optical flow is the special effects team tracing movements. Video classification is the reviewer labeling the genre. Tracking is the focus puller keeping the camera locked on the star!

cards.title: Deploying the complete pipeline
cards.body:
In a real-world warehouse, a complete system might look like:
• YOLOv8 detects packages.
• DeepSORT tracks their movement paths.
• An I3D model classifies actions like 'scanned', 'loaded', or 'dropped'.

cards.title: No single model rules them all
cards.body: Don't fall into the trap of thinking you should always use the most complex model. High-accuracy models like I3D are heavy and slow. For edge devices (like cameras or drones), lightweight 3D CNNs or sparse Lucas-Kanade flow are often far better.

cards.title: The Temporal Toolkit
cards.body: Choosing the right method for the task at hand:

left.text:
Learns features directly from raw frames
Processes space and time together
No pre-computation needed

right.text:
Extracts motion vectors explicitly
Bypasses 3D filter memory cost
Requires pre-processing computation

exercises.prompt: Which component predicts where an object will move next based on its current speed?
exercises.explain: Correct! The Kalman filter uses velocity equations to project the next position of a bounding box.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An inflated 2D network (I3D) repeats 2D filters along the time axis to reuse learned ImageNet weights.
exercises.explain: True. I3D bootstrap weights by replicating 2D image kernels along the temporal dimension.

exercises.sentence: If you need to analyze the speed of every single pixel in a high-resolution frame, you need a ___ optical flow method.
exercises.explain: Dense optical flow methods calculate motion vectors for all pixels in the frame, rather than just select keypoints.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks is best solved using a spatiotemporal model?

options.text: Identifying if a video clip shows a person swimming or running
Counting the number of static chairs in a room
Detecting text boundaries on a scanned invoice page

exercises.explain: Classifying swimming vs. running requires tracking coordinates and motion patterns over time, requiring a spatiotemporal representation.

exercises.prompt: You are building a high-performance video analyzer for sports broadcasting. You have infinite GPU resources. Which setup will give you the absolute highest accuracy?

options.text: A Two-Stream network combining an inflated 3D CNN (I3D) on RGB frames and another on dense optical flow, paired with DeepSORT tracking.
A frame-by-frame 2D LeNet classification model with no temporal tracking.
A sparse Lucas-Kanade keypoint tracker running on grayscale video with no CNN features.
A single Conv2D layer on the very first frame of the video.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Leveraging pre-trained 3D architectures on both appearance (RGB) and motion (optical flow), combined with appearance-based object tracking, delivers the state-of-the-art in spatiotemporal accuracy. Phase 13: Video & Temporal Vision is complete. Next up: Computer Vision in Production.
